zones.name: Primary Flight Display
zones.label: PFD
zones.description: Core AI surfaces — where you fly

instruments.name: Agent Mode
instruments.description: Autonomous agentic coding loop that iterates on multi-file edits, runs terminal commands, and self-corrects. The AI plans, implements, tests, and fixes — you review the result.
instruments.shortDescription: Autonomous multi-file coding with terminal access
capabilities: Multi-file editing across project
Terminal command execution
Self-correction on errors
Checkpoint creation for undo
MCP tool integration
Custom agent definitions via .github/copilot-agents.yml
Web search for current documentation

squawkCodes.title: Context Window Limits
squawkCodes.description: Agent may lose track of earlier changes in very long sessions. Break large tasks into smaller iterations.

squawkCodes.title: Terminal Safety
squawkCodes.description: Agent executes real terminal commands. Review proposed commands before approving, especially destructive operations like rm -rf.

aspects: Executes terminal commands
File system write access
MCP tool invocation
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Review tool permissions in enterprise settings. Agent respects .github/copilot-instructions.md guardrails.

instruments.proTip: Start with a detailed plan prompt ('Plan how to refactor X, then implement it') rather than jumping straight to implementation. Agent Mode performs best when it understands the full scope upfront.

mermaidDiagrams.title: Agent Mode Loop
mermaidDiagrams.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant A as Agent
    participant F as Files
    participant T as Terminal
    U->>A: Describe task
    A->>A: Create plan
    loop Until task complete
        A->>F: Edit files
        A->>T: Run commands
        T-->>A: Output/errors
        alt Errors found
            A->>A: Analyze & fix
        end
    end
    A-->>U: Present result + diff
    U->>A: Approve / request changes

codeExamples.title: Agent Mode Prompt
codeExamples.code:
@workspace Refactor the authentication module:
1. Extract JWT validation into a separate middleware
2. Add refresh token rotation
3. Write unit tests for both
4. Update the README with the new auth flow

codeExamples.title: Custom Agent Definition
codeExamples.code:
# .github/copilot-agents.yml
agents:
  - name: "security-reviewer"
    description: "Reviews code for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities"
    instructions: |
      Focus on: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, auth bypass.
      Check all user inputs are validated.
      Flag any hardcoded secrets.

instruments.name: @workspace
instruments.description: Codebase-wide context for Copilot Chat. In VS Code, workspace context is now provided implicitly — the @workspace prefix is optional. Copilot searches across all files in your workspace to answer questions about project structure, dependencies, and code relationships.
instruments.shortDescription: Codebase-wide context for Copilot Chat
capabilities: Semantic code search across workspace
Symbol and reference resolution
Project structure understanding
Cross-file dependency analysis
Natural language codebase Q&A

squawkCodes.title: Large Repo Indexing
squawkCodes.description: Very large repositories (>10k files) may take several minutes to fully index. Answers may be incomplete until indexing finishes.

squawkCodes.title: Ignored Files
squawkCodes.description: Files in .gitignore are excluded from workspace search. If you need context from generated files, they won't be found.

securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Workspace search respects .gitignore and content exclusion settings.

instruments.proTip: Use @workspace for broad questions ('How does authentication work in this project?') and @file for specific file context. Combining both narrows the search effectively.

mermaidDiagrams.title: How @workspace Resolves Context
mermaidDiagrams.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Q[User Question] --> I[Workspace Index]
    I --> S1[Semantic Search]
    I --> S2[Symbol Lookup]
    I --> S3[File Structure]
    S1 --> R[Ranked Results]
    S2 --> R
    S3 --> R
    R --> C[Context Window]
    C --> A[AI Response]
    style Q fill:#00d4ff,color:#000
    style A fill:#00d4ff,color:#000

codeExamples.title: Workspace Queries

instruments.name: Code Review
instruments.description: AI-powered pull request reviewer that analyzes diffs for bugs, security issues, and style problems. Leaves inline comments with suggested fixes directly on your PR.
instruments.shortDescription: AI code review on pull requests
capabilities: Automated PR review comments
Security vulnerability detection
Bug pattern identification
Style and best practice suggestions
Custom review instructions via copilot-instructions.md
Configurable via repository settings

squawkCodes.title: Large PR Noise
squawkCodes.description: Very large PRs (>500 lines changed) may produce many low-value comments. Keep PRs focused for best review quality.

squawkCodes.title: False Positives
squawkCodes.description: AI may flag correct code as problematic. Dismiss irrelevant comments to train the system.

squawkCodes.title: Custom Instruction Limit
squawkCodes.description: Copilot Code Review only reads the first 4,000 characters of any custom instruction file (.github/copilot-instructions.md).

aspects: Detects common security vulnerabilities
Flags credential exposure
Identifies injection risks
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Complements but does not replace SAST/DAST tooling. Use alongside GitHub Advanced Security for full coverage.

instruments.proTip: Add a copilot-instructions.md with review-specific guidelines ('Always flag raw SQL queries', 'Enforce error handling on all API calls') to focus reviews on what matters to your team.

mermaidDiagrams.title: Code Review Flow
mermaidDiagrams.diagram:
flowchart TB
    PR[Pull Request Created] --> T[Trigger: Auto or Manual]
    T --> D[Analyze Diff]
    D --> B{Issues Found?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Post Inline Comments]
    B -->|No| OK[Approve Review]
    C --> S[Suggest Fixes]
    S --> Dev[Developer Reviews]
    Dev -->|Accept| A[Apply Suggestion]
    Dev -->|Dismiss| DIS[Feedback Loop]
    style PR fill:#ffaa00,color:#000
    style OK fill:#00ff88,color:#000

codeExamples.title: Review Instructions
codeExamples.code:
<!-- .github/copilot-instructions.md -->
## Code Review Focus Areas
- Flag any SQL query that doesn't use parameterized statements
- Ensure all API endpoints have proper authentication checks
- Check that error responses don't leak internal details
- Verify new dependencies are from trusted sources

instruments.name: /explain
instruments.description: Slash command that provides detailed explanations of selected code. Breaks down complex logic, explains patterns, and describes what each section does in plain language.
instruments.shortDescription: Explain selected code in plain language
capabilities: Line-by-line code explanation
Pattern and idiom identification
Complexity analysis
Dependency chain explanation
Context-aware explanations using surrounding code

squawkCodes.title: Selection Size
squawkCodes.description: Very large selections (>200 lines) may produce superficial explanations. Select focused sections for deeper analysis.

instruments.proTip: Select just the confusing part, not the entire file. /explain works best on focused selections — a single function, a regex pattern, or a complex conditional chain.

mermaidDiagrams.title: Slash Commands Overview
mermaidDiagrams.diagram:
flowchart LR
    U[Select Code] --> SC{Slash Command}
    SC --> E[/explain - Understand]
    SC --> F[/fix - Repair]
    SC --> D[/doc - Document]
    SC --> T[/tests - Test]
    E --> R[Detailed Explanation]
    style E fill:#e0e0e0,color:#000
    style SC fill:#e0e0e0,color:#000

codeExamples.title: Using /explain
codeExamples.code:
# Select a complex regex, then type:
/explain

# Or ask about specific aspects:
/explain What is the time complexity of this function?

# Works great for unfamiliar codebases:
/explain How does this middleware chain work?

instruments.name: Model Context Protocol
instruments.description: Open protocol for connecting Copilot to external tools and data sources. MCP servers expose tools that Agent Mode can invoke — databases, APIs, design systems, deployment pipelines, and more.
instruments.shortDescription: Connect Copilot to external tools via open protocol
capabilities: Connect to external APIs and databases
Custom tool definitions
Bidirectional data flow
Server discovery and configuration
Works with Agent Mode for autonomous tool use
Community server ecosystem

squawkCodes.title: Trust Boundary
squawkCodes.description: MCP servers execute with local permissions. Only install servers from trusted sources — a malicious server could access your file system.

aspects: External tool execution
Network access to APIs
File system access via servers
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Audit installed MCP servers regularly. Use allowlists in enterprise environments. Servers run locally with user permissions.

instruments.proTip: Start with official MCP servers (filesystem, GitHub) before building custom ones. The ecosystem at mcp.so has hundreds of community servers for databases, cloud providers, and SaaS tools.

mermaidDiagrams.title: MCP Architecture
mermaidDiagrams.diagram:
flowchart TB
    C[Copilot / Agent Mode] --> MCP[MCP Protocol]
    MCP --> S1[Database Server]
    MCP --> S2[GitHub Server]
    MCP --> S3[Cloud API Server]
    MCP --> S4[Custom Server]
    S1 --> D1[(PostgreSQL)]
    S2 --> D2[GitHub API]
    S3 --> D3[AWS / Azure]
    S4 --> D4[Your Tools]
    style C fill:#aa88ff,color:#000
    style MCP fill:#aa88ff,color:#000

instruments.name: Copilot Chat
instruments.shortDescription: Conversational AI assistant for code questions, generation, and debugging
capabilities: Three conversation modes: Ask (read-only exploration), Plan (structured implementation planning), and Agent (autonomous code modification with terminal commands)
Context-aware slash commands, chat participants (@github for web search), and chat variables (#filename for file references)
Image attachment support (JPEG, PNG, GIF, WEBP) for analyzing screenshots, mockups, flowcharts, and error messages
Subagents: isolated agents with independent context windows for delegated specialized tasks
Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration to extend chat with external tools and data sources
Inline chat (Cmd+i / Ctrl+i) for quick edits without leaving the editor

squawkCodes.title: Organization Lockout
squawkCodes.description: Enterprise/org admins can disable Copilot Chat entirely or restrict Agent mode access. If chat is unresponsive, check org-level Copilot policy settings.

squawkCodes.title: Image Blindness
squawkCodes.description: Image attachment requires org-level feature enablement for Business/Enterprise plans. Individual plan users have it by default.

squawkCodes.title: Premium Request Multiplier
squawkCodes.description: Each user prompt in Agent mode counts as one premium request multiplied by the model's multiplier. Tool calls and follow-up actions do NOT incur additional charges.

codeExamples.title: Using Plan mode to architect a feature
codeExamples.code:
I need to add user authentication to this Express.js app
using JWT tokens. The app currently has no auth.

Plan the implementation including middleware,
login/register routes, and token refresh logic.

instruments.proTip: Use chat variables like #filename to reference specific files, and type @ to invoke specialized participants like @github for web search. Copilot can auto-select the right participant, but explicit invocation gives more predictable results.

aspects: Repository custom instructions are auto-included in context — review .github/copilot-instructions.md for unintended prompt injection vectors
Agent mode can execute terminal commands autonomously; user approval may be required depending on IDE settings
MCP server connections extend Copilot's reach to external services — each integration is an additional trust boundary
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Enterprise admins can restrict agent mode and MCP access via org-level policies. Chat responses are not stored by GitHub for training on Business/Enterprise plans.

instruments.name: Copilot Edits
instruments.shortDescription: Multi-file editing mode with diff preview and apply workflow
capabilities: Edit Mode: controlled multi-file editing where you choose which files Copilot can modify, review diffs per turn, and accept/reject each change
Agent Mode: autonomous editing where Copilot determines files to modify, runs terminal commands, and iterates until the task is complete
Plan Mode integration: create detailed implementation plans before executing edits, with option to hand off plan to agent
MCP server support in Agent Mode for integrating external tools during autonomous editing
Diff-based review: all changes shown as diffs that can be accepted, rejected, or iterated on before saving

squawkCodes.title: Edit Mode IDE Lock
squawkCodes.description: Edit mode is only available in VS Code and JetBrains IDEs. Visual Studio supports Agent mode but NOT Edit mode.

squawkCodes.title: Agent Mode Org Block
squawkCodes.description: Enterprise administrators can restrict agent mode access at the org level. If the Agent dropdown option is missing, check your org's Copilot policy.

squawkCodes.title: No Undo Stack
squawkCodes.description: Once you accept edits, they are applied to your files. Use version control (git) as your safety net — commit before large edit sessions.

codeExamples.title: Multi-file refactor in Edit Mode
codeExamples.code:
Refactor both files to use a shared BaseController class.
Extract common error handling and response formatting
into the base class. Keep the existing API contracts identical.

instruments.proTip: Use Edit mode (not Agent mode) when you want guardrails — it forces you to explicitly add files to the working set, preventing Copilot from touching unexpected files. Switch to Agent mode only when you genuinely want Copilot to discover and modify files on its own.

aspects: Agent mode can execute arbitrary terminal commands in your local environment
Edit mode provides a safer alternative with explicit file scoping and per-change review
MCP integrations in agent mode may access external services with your credentials
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Agent mode terminal execution is sandboxed to the IDE's terminal. Enterprise orgs can disable agent mode entirely via policy.

instruments.name: Inline Completions
instruments.shortDescription: Real-time code suggestions as you type, accepted with Tab
capabilities: Ghost text completions: dimmed inline suggestions appear at cursor position, accepted with Tab, rejected with Esc
Comment-driven generation: describe desired functionality in a code comment and Copilot suggests the implementing code
Partial acceptance: accept word-by-word (Cmd+Right / Ctrl+Right) or line-by-line for granular control
Alternative suggestions: cycle through multiple completion options (Option+] / Alt+] for next, Option+[ / Alt+[ for previous)
Multi-suggestion panel: open a dedicated tab showing all alternatives at once (Ctrl+Enter)

squawkCodes.title: Public Code Filter Active
squawkCodes.description: Duplication detection may silently suppress suggestions that match publicly available code. Check Settings > Copilot > 'Suggestions matching public code' on GitHub.com.

squawkCodes.title: Quota Exhaustion (Free Tier)
squawkCodes.description: Copilot Free plan has a monthly completions quota. When exhausted, suggestions stop until the next billing cycle.

squawkCodes.title: Xcode Double Suggestion
squawkCodes.description: Xcode's native predictive text completion can conflict with Copilot suggestions. Disable Xcode's native predictive completions to avoid confusion.

codeExamples.title: Comment-driven function generation

instruments.proTip: If you're getting sparse suggestions, check if duplication detection is enabled (GitHub.com > Settings > Copilot). This setting blocks suggestions matching public code, which can significantly reduce suggestion volume in common patterns.

aspects: Public code matching: Copilot checks suggestions against publicly available code and can discard matches, configurable per org
Code sent to GitHub servers for processing includes surrounding context from open files
Suggestions may inadvertently include patterns with security anti-patterns
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Orgs can enforce the 'block suggestions matching public code' policy. Business/Enterprise telemetry data is not retained for model training.

instruments.name: Next Edit Suggestions
instruments.shortDescription: Proactive suggestions for the next logical edit based on recent changes
capabilities: Predicts the location of your next likely edit based on changes you are currently making, then suggests a completion at that location
Gutter arrow indicator: an arrow in the editor gutter shows where a predicted edit is available, direction indicates above or below
Tab-Tab workflow: press Tab to navigate to the predicted edit location, press Tab again to accept the suggestion
Pattern propagation: after making a change in one location, NES predicts the same type of change elsewhere (e.g., renaming a variable in all usages)

squawkCodes.title: Limited IDE Coverage
squawkCodes.description: NES is GA only in VS Code, Xcode, and Eclipse. NOT available in JetBrains, Visual Studio, or Neovim.

squawkCodes.title: Requires Active Editing
squawkCodes.description: NES only activates when you are actively making edits. It does not trigger on passive file browsing or reading.

squawkCodes.title: Gutter Arrow Blindness
squawkCodes.description: The gutter arrow indicator can be easy to miss, especially with dense gutter decorations (breakpoints, git markers).

codeExamples.title: NES: consistent rename propagation

instruments.proTip: The arrow direction tells you whether the predicted edit is above or below your current viewport — useful in large files so you know which way to expect the jump. Hover over the gutter arrow for keyboard shortcuts.

aspects: NES operates on the same context model as inline completions — no additional data is sent beyond what inline completions already use
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Same data handling and retention policies as inline completions.

instruments.name: Coding Agent
instruments.shortDescription: Cloud-based headless agent that works on issues autonomously via GitHub
capabilities: Fully autonomous development: researches repository, creates plan, writes code, runs tests/linters, and iterates — all in an ephemeral GitHub Actions environment
Multiple trigger points: GitHub Issues, agents panel on GitHub.com, Copilot Chat in IDEs, GitHub CLI, and @copilot mentions on PRs
Automated git workflow: creates branches, writes commit messages, pushes changes, and opens pull requests — no local checkout needed
Custom agents: create specialized agents for different task types with domain-specific instructions and tools via copilot-agents.yml
MCP and hooks integration: extend capabilities with external tools (Jira, Slack, Linear, Azure Boards) and custom shell commands

squawkCodes.title: Single-Repo Constraint
squawkCodes.description: The coding agent can only make changes in ONE repository per task. Cross-repo changes require separate tasks.

squawkCodes.title: Branch Protection Conflict
squawkCodes.description: Repository rulesets or branch protection that restrict commit authors will block the agent. Ensure Copilot's commit identity is allowed.

squawkCodes.title: Content Exclusions Ignored
squawkCodes.description: The coding agent does NOT respect Copilot content exclusion settings. Files excluded from Copilot may still be read/modified by the agent.

squawkCodes.title: GitHub-Hosted Only
squawkCodes.description: Only works with GitHub-hosted repositories. GitHub Enterprise Server (GHES) on-premises repos are not supported.

codeExamples.title: Assigning a task via GitHub Issue
codeExamples.code:
## Add rate limiting to /api/search

### Requirements
- Rate limiting middleware (100 req/min per API key)
- Return 429 with Retry-After header when exceeded
- Add unit tests for the rate limiter
- Update API docs in docs/api.md

/assign @copilot

instruments.proTip: The coding agent runs in GitHub Actions, so your monthly Actions minutes and premium request quotas apply. It works best on well-scoped, incremental tasks — avoid assigning broad 'rewrite the whole module' issues.

aspects: Runs in an ephemeral GitHub Actions environment with access to your repository code
Organizations can customize firewall rules controlling which domains/URLs the agent can reach
Content exclusion settings are NOT respected — the agent may access files intended to be excluded
Custom hooks allow execution of arbitrary shell commands at agent execution checkpoints
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Enterprise admins must explicitly enable the feature. Repository owners can opt out specific repos. All agent actions are logged via GitHub Actions. PRs always require human review before merge.

instruments.name: @file
instruments.shortDescription: Attach specific files as context to Copilot Chat conversations
capabilities: Explicit file context attachment
Multiple file references
Focused context for precise answers

squawkCodes.title: Token window still applies
squawkCodes.description: Attaching very large files via #file may exceed Copilot's context window, causing truncation. Prefer attaching focused, smaller files or use #selection for specific code regions.

squawkCodes.title: File must be in the workspace
squawkCodes.description: The #file context variable only resolves files within the currently open VS Code workspace. Files outside the workspace or binary files are not supported.

squawkCodes.title: Sensitive file content sent to Copilot
squawkCodes.description: Any file attached via #file has its full contents sent to the Copilot backend. Avoid attaching files containing secrets, credentials, API keys, or .env files.

codeExamples.title: Attach a file for targeted code review
codeExamples.code: #file:src/utils/auth.ts Review this authentication helper for security issues and suggest improvements.

codeExamples.title: Reference multiple files for cross-file analysis
codeExamples.code: #file:src/models/user.ts #file:src/routes/userRoutes.ts Explain how the User model is used in the route handlers and check for missing validation.

codeExamples.title: Generate tests based on a specific file
codeExamples.code: #file:src/services/paymentService.ts Write unit tests for this payment service covering edge cases like failed transactions and currency conversion.

aspects: File contents are transmitted to GitHub Copilot's cloud service
Sensitive files (secrets, credentials) should never be attached via #file
Content exclusion policies can block specific files at the org level
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Copilot for Business and Enterprise offer data retention controls. Content exclusion settings at the org level can prevent specific file patterns from being sent.

instruments.proTip: Combine #file with #selection for surgical precision: attach a full file for context, then highlight a specific function and ask Copilot to refactor just that selection in the context of the whole module.

instruments.name: @github
instruments.shortDescription: Access GitHub data like issues, PRs, and repos in Copilot Chat
capabilities: Dynamically selects appropriate GitHub skills based on the content of your question
Web search capability via natural language or explicit #web variable for real-time information
Repository operations including create_branch, create_or_update_file, push_files, and merge_pull_request
Query GitHub data: open PRs, issues, users, and repository information
Supports MCP skills for extensible GitHub operations
Discover available skills by asking '@github What skills are available?'

squawkCodes.title: Preview in Visual Studio
squawkCodes.description: @github is in public preview in Visual Studio and subject to change. Feature availability and supported skills may vary between releases.

squawkCodes.title: Skill Auto-Selection
squawkCodes.description: Copilot dynamically selects skills based on question content, which means it may not always invoke the intended skill. Use explicit syntax when precision matters.

squawkCodes.title: VS Code Primary Support
squawkCodes.description: Chat participants using @ syntax are primarily documented for VS Code. Other IDEs may not support the @github participant.

codeExamples.title: Search the web for current information
codeExamples.code: @github #web What is the latest LTS version of Node.js?

instruments.proTip: You can invoke web search two ways: naturally ('Search the web to find...') or explicitly with the #web variable. The #web variable is more reliable for ensuring web search is actually triggered.

aspects: Can perform write operations (create branches, push files, merge PRs) on your repositories
Web search results are processed by the model and could influence code suggestions
Requires appropriate GitHub permissions for repository operations
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Write operations via @github act with your GitHub permissions. Review suggested operations before confirming, especially in production repositories.

instruments.name: @terminal
instruments.shortDescription: Include terminal output as context for Copilot Chat
capabilities: Terminal output as context
Error message analysis
Build output understanding

squawkCodes.title: VS Code only
squawkCodes.description: The @terminal chat participant is VS Code-specific. JetBrains IDEs do not support @terminal — users must manually copy terminal output into chat.

squawkCodes.title: Limited terminal buffer
squawkCodes.description: Only the most recent terminal output is captured. Long-running commands with verbose output may be partially truncated.

squawkCodes.title: Terminal may contain secrets
squawkCodes.description: Terminal output can contain environment variables, connection strings, API responses with tokens, or database query results. All captured terminal content is sent to Copilot.

codeExamples.title: Debug a build error from terminal output
codeExamples.code: @terminal Explain the error in my last command and suggest a fix.

codeExamples.title: Analyze test failures from terminal output
codeExamples.code: @terminal My test suite just failed. Summarize which tests failed and suggest likely causes based on the error output.

codeExamples.title: Suggest next steps from terminal history
codeExamples.code: @terminal I just cloned this repo and ran the setup. What should I do next based on the terminal output?

aspects: Terminal output often contains environment variables and auth tokens
Shell history may reveal server addresses and infrastructure details
Content exclusion policies do not cover terminal buffer content
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Organizations should train developers to clear sensitive terminal output before using @terminal. Content exclusion policies only cover file-based content, not terminal buffers.

instruments.proTip: Run your failing command, then immediately use @terminal before running anything else — the context is freshest right after the error. Keep one terminal dedicated to the failing process so @terminal picks up the relevant output without noise.

instruments.name: @vscode
instruments.shortDescription: Ask questions about VS Code features, settings, and extensions
capabilities: Has context about VS Code commands, features, and settings
Helps discover and configure VS Code settings like word wrap, themes, and keybindings
Provides guidance on VS Code extension development via the Extension API
Answers questions about VS Code keyboard shortcuts and command palette actions

squawkCodes.title: VS Code Exclusive
squawkCodes.description: @vscode is exclusively a VS Code chat participant. It provides no value in other IDEs and is not available outside VS Code.

squawkCodes.title: Editor Knowledge Only
squawkCodes.description: @vscode knows about VS Code the editor, not your codebase. Don't confuse it with @workspace which understands your project code.

codeExamples.title: Discover VS Code settings
codeExamples.code:
@vscode How do I enable word wrapping
and set the tab size to 2 spaces?

instruments.proTip: Use @vscode for questions about the editor itself, not your code. It knows VS Code internals — ask it about obscure settings, multi-cursor tricks, or how to configure tasks.json. For code questions, use @workspace instead.

aspects: Limited security surface as it primarily deals with editor configuration
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Minimal security relevance. @vscode deals with editor configuration, not code or repository data.

instruments.name: Codebase Indexing
instruments.shortDescription: Background indexing engine that powers workspace-aware features
capabilities: Semantic code search finds code by meaning rather than exact keywords, invoked via #codebase
Three index types: Remote (GitHub-hosted), Local (on-machine semantic index), and Basic (simpler algorithms for large codebases)
Automatic indexing runs in the background — initial indexing takes up to 60 seconds, re-indexing updates within seconds
Indexes all files except those in .gitignore, files.exclude settings, and binary files
Agents combine semantic search with text search, grep, file search, and Find All References for comprehensive understanding

squawkCodes.title: Initial Indexing Delay
squawkCodes.description: First-time indexing of a large repository can take up to 60 seconds. Semantic search queries during this period may return incomplete results.

squawkCodes.title: GHES Not Supported
squawkCodes.description: Remote indexing supports GitHub.com and GitHub Enterprise Cloud but NOT GitHub Enterprise Server. GHES users are limited to local indexing.

squawkCodes.title: Binary Files Excluded
squawkCodes.description: Binary files (images, PDFs, compiled assets) are never indexed. .gitignore'd files are also excluded from the workspace index.

codeExamples.title: Search codebase for error handling patterns
codeExamples.code:
#codebase How is error handling implemented in the
payment processing service? Show me all retry logic
and fallback patterns.

instruments.proTip: Even without semantic indexing, Copilot agents still function using text search, grep, and language intelligence. But for meaning-based queries ('find code that handles authentication') rather than keyword queries ('find AuthService class'), semantic indexing makes a dramatic difference.

aspects: Indexed repository data is NOT used for model training, per official documentation
Enterprise/organization owners can define content exclusions that filter indexed data before it reaches Copilot Chat
Indexed data may be stored both locally and on remote servers depending on index type
.gitignore is bypassed if you have a file open or have text selected within an ignored file
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Copilot will not use indexed repository data for model training. Enterprise admins can configure content exclusion policies. Note the .gitignore bypass caveat for open/selected files.

instruments.name: Copilot Autofix
instruments.shortDescription: Automatically generates fix suggestions for security vulnerabilities found by code scanning
capabilities: Automatically generates AI-powered fix suggestions for CodeQL code scanning alerts on pull requests
Supports C#, C/C++, Go, Java/Kotlin, Swift, JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Ruby, and Rust
Uses GPT-5.1 to translate alert descriptions and code locations into concrete code change suggestions
Provides explanatory text alongside suggested code changes to help developers understand the fix
Enabled by default for all repositories using CodeQL — no Copilot subscription required
Configurable (enabled/disabled) at enterprise, organization, or repository levels

squawkCodes.title: Partial Fix
squawkCodes.description: Autofix may only partially address a vulnerability due to limited visibility into the full codebase context.

squawkCodes.title: Fabricated Dependencies
squawkCodes.description: Suggested fixes may reference fabricated or insecure dependency versions — always verify package names and versions.

squawkCodes.title: Non-Deterministic
squawkCodes.description: The LLM may produce different suggestions for identical alerts across runs — results are not guaranteed to be reproducible.

codeExamples.title: Autofix workflow on a pull request
codeExamples.code:
# No config needed — Autofix activates automatically
# when CodeQL code scanning is enabled.
#
# To disable at org level:
# Settings > Code security > Code scanning
#   > Copilot Autofix > Disable
#
# Autofix suggestions appear inline on PR alerts
# as commit-ready code changes.

instruments.proTip: Always run CI tests after committing an Autofix suggestion — the fix may resolve the security alert but could introduce semantic changes that break existing functionality.

aspects: Directly remediates security vulnerabilities found by CodeQL static analysis
Data sent to GPT-5.1 includes code snippets around alert locations
Customer data is NOT used for LLM training — governed by Advanced Security terms
Generated fixes may themselves introduce new vulnerabilities — human review mandatory
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: No Copilot subscription required for public repos. Private/internal repos require GitHub Code Security license.

instruments.name: Security Scanning
instruments.shortDescription: Code scanning with CodeQL and third-party tools to find vulnerabilities
capabilities: Uses CodeQL analysis engine to automatically find security vulnerabilities and coding errors
Supports 12 languages: C/C++, C#, Go, Java, Kotlin, JS, TS, Python, Ruby, Rust, Swift, and GitHub Actions YAML
Default setup (one-click) and advanced setup (custom GitHub Actions workflow with third-party tools)
Integrates with pull requests to surface alerts before merge, with optional merge protection rules
Interoperable with third-party SARIF-compliant tools — results uploadable from any CI system
Organization-wide security overview for monitoring results across all repositories

squawkCodes.title: SARIF Open Standard
squawkCodes.description: Code scanning uses SARIF — you can integrate any SARIF-compliant tool, not just CodeQL.

squawkCodes.title: Actions Minutes
squawkCodes.description: Code scanning consumes GitHub Actions minutes — private repos require sufficient Actions quota and a Code Security license.

squawkCodes.title: Merge Protection
squawkCodes.description: Configure merge protection rules to prevent PRs with unresolved scanning alerts from being merged.

codeExamples.title: Advanced CodeQL scanning workflow
codeExamples.code:
name: 'CodeQL Analysis'
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'
jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      security-events: write
    strategy:
      matrix:
        language: ['javascript', 'python']
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
        with:
          languages: ${{ matrix.language }}
      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v3

instruments.proTip: Start with default setup for immediate coverage, then graduate to advanced setup when you need custom CodeQL query suites or third-party tool integration via SARIF uploads.

aspects: Core security analysis tool — identifies vulnerabilities via static analysis
CodeQL treats code as data, querying it for vulnerability patterns across control and data flow
Supports variant analysis to find similar vulnerabilities across multiple repositories
Organization security overview provides centralized vulnerability monitoring
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Free for public repos. Private repos require GitHub Code Security license. SARIF standard enables integration with compliance toolchains.

instruments.name: Agent Self-Review
instruments.shortDescription: Agent Mode reviews its own changes before presenting to the user
capabilities: Coding agent autonomously creates PRs; auto-review rulesets assign Copilot to review them
Creates a feedback loop where AI-generated code is reviewed by AI before human review
Review comments appear as standard PR comments that developers can accept or dismiss
Configurable via rulesets to review draft PRs (catching issues before human review is requested)
Reviews complete in under 30 seconds and leave Comment-type reviews (never Approve)

squawkCodes.title: Not an Approval
squawkCodes.description: Copilot code review always leaves a 'Comment' review, never 'Approve' or 'Request changes' — it cannot satisfy required review rules.

squawkCodes.title: Human Review Required
squawkCodes.description: Agent self-review supplements but does not replace human review — developers must validate all agent-generated code.

squawkCodes.title: Review Hallucination
squawkCodes.description: Review comments may flag non-existent problems or misunderstand agent-generated code patterns.

codeExamples.title: Configure auto-review for agent PRs
codeExamples.code:
Settings > Rules > Rulesets > New branch ruleset
  Name: auto-review-agent-prs
  Enforcement: Active
  Target branches: Include all branches
  Branch rules:
    [x] Automatically request Copilot code review
    [x] Review new pushes
    [x] Review draft pull requests

instruments.description: Not a standalone feature — the combination of the Copilot coding agent creating PRs and automatic Copilot code review rulesets reviewing those PRs. When both are active, Copilot effectively reviews its own generated code.
instruments.proTip: Enable 'Review draft pull requests' in your auto-review ruleset so Copilot reviews agent-generated PRs while they are still drafts — catching issues before any human reviewer is assigned.

aspects: Adds automated review layer to catch security issues in AI-generated code before human review
Review may miss complex security issues — not a substitute for security-focused human review
Custom instructions can include security checklists for reviews
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Requires Copilot Enterprise or Pro+. Copilot review reads only the first 4,000 characters of custom instruction files.

instruments.name: Auto Review Assignment
instruments.shortDescription: Automatically assigns Copilot as a reviewer on pull requests
capabilities: Automatic reviewer assignment
Configurable trigger rules
Team workflow integration

squawkCodes.title: Not a replacement for human review
squawkCodes.description: Copilot auto-review provides AI-generated feedback but cannot approve or merge PRs. Human reviewers are still required for final approval in protected branch workflows.

squawkCodes.title: Org-level configuration only
squawkCodes.description: This feature is configured at the organization level by admins. Individual users cannot enable or disable it for their own repositories without org permissions.

squawkCodes.title: Sensitive code exposure
squawkCodes.description: Copilot will analyze all code in reviewed PRs. Ensure your organization's data handling policies permit AI analysis of the codebase.

codeExamples.title: Enabling Copilot as automatic PR reviewer
codeExamples.code: Navigate to: Organization Settings → Copilot → Policies → Pull Request Reviews → Enable 'Automatically request Copilot review on pull requests'

aspects: Copilot reviews may flag security vulnerabilities like SQL injection or hardcoded secrets
Review comments are visible to all PR participants, becoming part of the audit trail
Does not replace dedicated SAST/DAST tooling
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Organizations subject to SOC 2 or ISO 27001 should document that AI-assisted reviews supplement, not replace, mandatory human security reviews.

instruments.proTip: Combine auto-review-assign with branch protection rules that still require at least one human approval — Copilot catches style and bug issues early so human reviewers can focus on architecture and business logic.

instruments.name: PR Summaries
instruments.shortDescription: Auto-generated pull request descriptions summarizing changes
capabilities: Generates AI-powered summaries of pull request changes to help reviewers understand the diff
Available in the PR description editor and comment fields via the Copilot icon menu
Works on both new and existing pull requests on GitHub.com
Summarizes the nature and purpose of code changes across all files in the diff
Users can edit and augment the generated summary before publishing

squawkCodes.title: Blank Description Best
squawkCodes.description: Copilot ignores existing PR description text when generating summaries — start with a blank description for best results.

squawkCodes.title: Supplement Required
squawkCodes.description: Generated summaries should be reviewed and supplemented with business context, testing notes, and deployment considerations.

squawkCodes.title: GitHub.com Only
squawkCodes.description: PR summaries are only available on GitHub.com — no IDE or CLI integration exists for this feature.

codeExamples.title: Generating a PR summary
codeExamples.code:
1. Navigate to your pull request on GitHub.com
2. Click the Copilot icon in the description field
3. Select 'Summary' from the dropdown
4. Review the generated summary
5. Add additional context or edit as needed
6. Publish the pull request

instruments.proTip: Start with a blank PR description before generating a summary — Copilot does not account for existing content, so pre-existing text may lead to redundant or conflicting descriptions.

aspects: PR summaries describe code changes but do not perform security analysis
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Available with Copilot subscription plans. Descriptive only — does not evaluate code security.

instruments.name: /fix
instruments.shortDescription: Propose a fix for problems in selected code or diagnostics
capabilities: Error fix suggestions
Diagnostic-based repairs
Quick fix generation

squawkCodes.title: Fixes are suggestions, not guarantees
squawkCodes.description: Copilot proposes a fix based on the visible code and error description. It may not account for runtime state, external dependencies, or side effects in other files.

squawkCodes.title: Vague prompts produce vague fixes
squawkCodes.description: Typing just '/fix' without selecting code or describing the error often yields generic refactoring rather than a targeted fix. Always select the problematic code and describe the symptom.

squawkCodes.title: Fix may mask deeper issues
squawkCodes.description: A suggested fix might suppress an error (e.g., adding a null check) without addressing the root cause, potentially hiding security-relevant bugs.

codeExamples.title: Fix a null reference error
codeExamples.code: /fix Why is this throwing a null reference exception?

codeExamples.title: Fix with selected code context
codeExamples.code: /fix The loop index is off by one and causes an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException

aspects: Fixes may introduce unsafe patterns such as broad exception swallowing
A fix that silences a security warning without resolving the vulnerability creates false safety
Always review suggested fixes against secure coding guidelines
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Treat AI-suggested fixes with the same review rigor as human-authored code. Audit logs should reflect that the change was AI-assisted.

instruments.proTip: Select the exact lines containing the bug before invoking /fix and include the error message verbatim — Copilot performs dramatically better with precise context than with an entire file and a vague complaint.

instruments.name: /doc
instruments.shortDescription: Generate documentation comments for selected code
capabilities: JSDoc/docstring generation
Parameter documentation
Return type documentation
Language-aware formatting

squawkCodes.title: Only available in Visual Studio and Xcode
squawkCodes.description: The /doc slash command is NOT available in VS Code or JetBrains IDEs. In those editors, use inline chat with a prompt like 'Add documentation comments to this function' instead.

squawkCodes.title: Generated docs may be inaccurate
squawkCodes.description: Copilot infers parameter purposes and return values from code patterns. Complex business logic or non-obvious side effects may be described incorrectly.

squawkCodes.title: Does not update existing docs
squawkCodes.description: If documentation already exists, /doc may duplicate or conflict with it rather than intelligently merging. Remove existing doc comments before regenerating.

codeExamples.title: Generate XML documentation for a C# method
codeExamples.code: /doc Generate documentation for the selected method including parameter descriptions and return value

codeExamples.title: Document a Swift class in Xcode
codeExamples.code: /doc Add documentation comments to this class explaining its purpose and public API

aspects: Documentation generation is read-only analysis
Generated docs could inadvertently describe internal security mechanisms in detail
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Review generated documentation to ensure it does not expose sensitive implementation details such as encryption algorithms or authentication flows.

instruments.proTip: In VS Code or JetBrains where /doc is unavailable, select your function and use inline chat with 'Write a JSDoc/docstring for this function' — you get the same result, just without the shortcut.

instruments.name: /tests
instruments.shortDescription: Generate unit tests for selected code
capabilities: Unit test generation
Framework-aware test scaffolding
Edge case coverage
Mock and stub generation

squawkCodes.title: Framework mismatch
squawkCodes.description: Copilot may generate tests using a different testing framework than your project uses (e.g., Jest instead of Vitest). Always specify your framework in the prompt.

squawkCodes.title: Assertions may be superficial
squawkCodes.description: Generated tests often verify that a function returns 'something' rather than testing specific business rules. Treat generated tests as a skeleton that needs assertion hardening.

squawkCodes.title: Missing dependency mocks
squawkCodes.description: Tests for code with external dependencies may fail immediately because Copilot did not generate the necessary mocks or test fixtures.

codeExamples.title: Generate unit tests for a selected function
codeExamples.code: /tests Generate unit tests for the selected function covering edge cases and error handling

codeExamples.title: Specify a testing framework
codeExamples.code: /tests Write tests using Jest with describe/it blocks and include mocks for the database dependency

aspects: Generated tests rarely include security-specific test cases
Test data may include hardcoded credentials or realistic PII
Passing AI-generated tests can create false coverage confidence
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Review AI-generated tests to ensure they do not contain hardcoded secrets or realistic personal data that would violate data protection policies.

instruments.proTip: After generating tests with /tests, immediately run them — roughly half will need adjustment. Fix failures first, then add edge cases Copilot missed, especially around null inputs, boundary values, and authorization checks.

instruments.name: /new
instruments.shortDescription: Scaffold new projects, files, or components from templates
capabilities: Project scaffolding
File template generation
Component boilerplate
Multi-file creation

squawkCodes.title: Outdated dependencies
squawkCodes.description: Scaffolded projects may reference outdated package versions or deprecated APIs. Always run 'npm audit' or equivalent and update dependencies.

squawkCodes.title: No workspace context
squawkCodes.description: /new creates files based on the prompt alone and does not consider your existing project structure, linting rules, or conventions.

squawkCodes.title: Insecure defaults possible
squawkCodes.description: Scaffolded projects may include permissive CORS settings, disabled CSRF protection, or debug mode enabled by default.

codeExamples.title: Scaffold a new Express API project
codeExamples.code: /new Create a Node.js Express API with TypeScript, a health check endpoint, and Jest configured for testing

codeExamples.title: Scaffold a React component
codeExamples.code: /new Create a React component for a sortable data table with pagination using TypeScript and CSS modules

aspects: Scaffolded projects may include overly permissive configurations (CORS '*', debug mode)
Generated package files may pin vulnerable dependency versions
Boilerplate auth code may use insecure defaults
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Treat scaffolded code as untrusted. Run dependency vulnerability scanning and configuration review before promoting beyond local dev.

instruments.proTip: Use /new to generate the boilerplate, then immediately compare against your team's project template — it's far faster to adjust a scaffold than to write one from scratch, but never ship it unreviewed.

instruments.name: /search
instruments.shortDescription: Search the web for up-to-date information from within Copilot Chat
capabilities: Web search integration
Current documentation lookup
Up-to-date API information
Bing-powered search results

squawkCodes.title: Workspace indexing required
squawkCodes.description: Semantic search quality depends on workspace indexing. In large or newly opened workspaces, results may be incomplete until indexing finishes.

squawkCodes.title: Not a replacement for exact search
squawkCodes.description: /search uses semantic understanding but may miss exact string matches. Use both /search and Ctrl+Shift+F for thorough results.

squawkCodes.title: Results may include false positives
squawkCodes.description: Semantic search can surface code that is conceptually similar but functionally unrelated.

codeExamples.title: Find authentication-related code
codeExamples.code: /search Where is the user authentication logic that validates JWT tokens?

codeExamples.title: Locate error handling patterns
codeExamples.code: /search Find all places where database connection errors are caught and retried

aspects: Search is a read-only operation
Useful for security audits — quickly locate all instances of crypto usage or input validation across a codebase
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Leverage /search during security reviews to locate patterns like raw SQL queries, hardcoded credentials, or disabled security checks.

instruments.proTip: Phrase your /search queries in terms of what the code does, not what it is named — 'where do we check if a user has admin privileges' finds more results than 'isAdmin function' because semantic search understands intent.

instruments.name: Feature Policies
instruments.shortDescription: Organization-level controls to enable or disable specific Copilot features
capabilities: Enable or disable Copilot features per organization
Control third-party coding agent access (Claude, Codex)
Enterprise-level policy enforcement with org override prevention
Manage MCP server availability for Copilot
Toggle preview features and user feedback collection

squawkCodes.title: Enterprise Override Lock
squawkCodes.description: If your enterprise has set explicit policies, organization admins cannot override them. Check enterprise settings first when a policy toggle appears greyed out.

squawkCodes.title: Third-Party Agent Control
squawkCodes.description: Enabling third-party coding agents (Claude, Codex) requires explicit org policy opt-in. These are disabled by default on Business and Enterprise plans.

codeExamples.title: Policy Configuration via REST API
codeExamples.code:
# Get current Copilot policies for an organization
curl -L \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/orgs/ORG/copilot/billing

instruments.description: Organization and enterprise-level policy controls that govern which Copilot features are available to members. Admins configure policies via Settings > Copilot > Policies, with enterprise settings taking precedence over org-level overrides.
instruments.proTip: Enterprise policies override org policies — if a feature is disabled at the enterprise level, org admins cannot re-enable it. Start with restrictive enterprise defaults and selectively open features per org.

aspects: Controls which AI features are accessible to developers
Prevents unauthorized agent or MCP server usage
Enterprise-level governance enforcement
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Use enterprise policies to enforce consistent AI governance across all organizations. Disable preview features in regulated environments.

instruments.name: Content Exclusion
instruments.shortDescription: Exclude specific files and repositories from being used as Copilot context
capabilities: File and directory exclusion via fnmatch glob patterns
Repository, organization, and enterprise-level configuration
Wildcard patterns for broad exclusion (e.g., '**/.env')
Propagation to IDEs within 30 minutes or on reload
Organization-wide rules across all repositories with '*' target

squawkCodes.title: Agent Mode Bypass
squawkCodes.description: Content exclusions are NOT enforced in Copilot CLI, cloud agent, or Agent Mode in IDEs. Sensitive files may still be read by agentic features.

squawkCodes.title: Propagation Delay
squawkCodes.description: Exclusion rules take up to 30 minutes to reach IDEs. Reload your editor or restart to force immediate pickup. Vim/Neovim refetches on each file open.

squawkCodes.title: Fnmatch Pattern Scope
squawkCodes.description: Patterns use fnmatch notation with case-insensitive matching. The '#' character starts a comment line — use quotes if your path contains '#'.

codeExamples.title: Organization Content Exclusion Config
codeExamples.code:
# Organization Settings > Copilot > Content exclusion
# Exclude secrets across ALL repos:
"*":
  - "**/.env"
  - "**/secrets.json"
  - "**/*.pem"

# Exclude entire internal repo:
my-org/internal-configs:
  - "**"

# Exclude specific paths in a repo:
my-org/api-server:
  - "/config/production/**"
  - "/scripts/deploy*"

instruments.description: Exclude specific files, directories, and repositories from being used as Copilot context. Configured at repository, organization, or enterprise level using fnmatch glob patterns. Excluded content receives no inline suggestions and is invisible to Copilot Chat.
instruments.proTip: Use the wildcard repository target '*' at the org level to exclude sensitive file patterns (like .env and *.pem) across every repository. Changes take up to 30 minutes to propagate — restart your IDE to force an immediate update.

aspects: Prevents sensitive files from leaking into AI context
Enforces data boundaries for compliance
Does NOT cover agentic features (CLI, cloud agent, Agent Mode)
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Critical for regulated environments. Exclude credentials, PII-containing configs, and proprietary algorithms. Note the agent mode gap — pair with feature policies to disable agent features if full exclusion is required.

instruments.name: IP Indemnity
instruments.shortDescription: Intellectual property indemnification for Copilot-generated code
capabilities: Legal defense against third-party IP infringement claims
Duplicate detection filter to block public code matches
Code referencing to surface original source and license info
Coverage for unmodified Copilot suggestions only
Liability cap removal for volume licensing customers (Aug 2025)

squawkCodes.title: Filter Required for Coverage
squawkCodes.description: IP indemnity only applies when the duplicate detection filter is set to 'Block'. Without it, indemnification does not cover generated suggestions.

squawkCodes.title: Unmodified Output Only
squawkCodes.description: Indemnity covers Copilot's suggestions as-is. If a developer substantially modifies the suggestion before using it, the indemnity protection may not apply.

squawkCodes.title: Plan Restriction
squawkCodes.description: Only Business and Enterprise plans include IP indemnity. Free, Pro, and Pro+ users have no indemnification coverage regardless of filter settings.

codeExamples.title: Enable Duplicate Detection Filter
codeExamples.code:
# Via REST API: set the code referencing / duplicate detection policy
curl -L -X PATCH \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $GITHUB_TOKEN" \
  https://api.github.com/orgs/ORG/copilot/billing \
  -d '{"public_code_suggestions":"block"}'

instruments.description: Intellectual property indemnification that protects Business and Enterprise customers against third-party copyright claims for Copilot's unmodified output. Requires the duplicate detection filter to be enabled. GitHub/Microsoft will defend covered customers in court.
instruments.proTip: IP indemnity only applies when the duplicate detection filter is set to 'Block'. Enable it in org policies under Copilot > Policies > Suggestions matching public code. Individual and Pro plans do NOT include indemnity coverage.

aspects: Legal protection against copyright infringement claims
Duplicate detection blocks suggestions matching public code
Code referencing surfaces license and attribution info
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Essential for enterprises in regulated industries. Enable the duplicate detection filter to qualify for indemnity. Review code referencing logs to track public code matches accepted by developers.

instruments.name: Audit Logs

capabilities: Track Copilot plan, policy, and license change events

squawkCodes.title: No Local Session Data
squawkCodes.description: Audit logs do not include prompts, completions, or chat messages sent locally from IDEs. Only server-side admin and agent events are recorded.

squawkCodes.title: 180-Day Retention Limit
squawkCodes.description: Audit log events are retained for 180 days only. Stream logs to a SIEM platform (Splunk, Sentinel, etc.) for long-term compliance retention.

squawkCodes.title: Enterprise-Level Only

codeExamples.title: Query Copilot Audit Events via API

curl -L \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" \

  "https://api.github.com/enterprises/ENTERPRISE/audit-log?phrase=action:copilot&per_page=50"

instruments.description: Enterprise audit logging for Copilot that records plan changes, policy modifications, license assignments, and agent activity. Logs are retained for 180 days and can be streamed to a SIEM for long-term retention. Does not capture local prompts or session content.
instruments.proTip: Audit logs do NOT capture local IDE prompts or suggestions — only admin actions and agent activity on GitHub.com. For local telemetry, configure custom hooks to send Copilot CLI events to your own logging service.

aspects: Tracks administrative changes to Copilot configuration
Monitors agent actions on GitHub.com
Supports compliance auditing and incident investigation
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Stream audit logs to a SIEM for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 evidence collection. Combine with usage metrics for comprehensive Copilot oversight. Local prompt data requires separate tooling.

instruments.name: Usage Metrics

capabilities: Seat assignment count and estimated monthly cost overview

instruments.proTip: Business tier ($19/seat) includes 300 premium requests per user — the same as Pro ($10/individual). The extra cost covers centralized management, policy controls, and audit logs that enterprises need.

squawkCodes.title: No inline completions
squawkCodes.description: GitHub Mobile Copilot is chat-only. It does not provide inline code completions, ghost text, or tab-to-accept suggestions.

squawkCodes.title: No agent mode or tool use
squawkCodes.description: Mobile Copilot cannot run terminal commands, edit files, or use agent mode workflows. It is limited to conversational Q&A.

squawkCodes.title: Reduced context window
squawkCodes.description: Mobile chat sessions may carry less context than IDE-based Copilot Chat. Very large repos or long conversations can lose earlier context.

squawkCodes.title: Repo not indexed
squawkCodes.description: If you ask about a specific repository but have not attached it, Copilot may give generic answers. Always attach the repo context explicitly.

codeExamples.title: Ask Copilot about a repository on mobile

aspects: Conversations may include sensitive code context sent over mobile networks
Device lock screen and notifications could expose code snippets
Shared or lost devices could expose Copilot chat history
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Ensure mobile device management (MDM) policies are in place if developers use Copilot on mobile with access to private repositories.

instruments.proTip: Attach a specific repo to your mobile Copilot conversation before asking questions — tap the repo reference button rather than just naming the repo in your prompt. This gives Copilot actual code search access instead of relying on training data.

squawkCodes.title: gh copilot Deprecated

instruments.proTip: The standalone 'copilot' command replaces the old 'gh copilot' extension (deprecated Oct 2025). Use --yolo to auto-approve all tool permissions in trusted environments, and --continue to resume your most recent session.

Directory trust boundaries with explicit user confirmation

instruments.name: copilot-instructions.md
instruments.shortDescription: Repository-level custom instructions that guide Copilot behavior
capabilities: Repository-level custom instructions via .github/copilot-instructions.md that automatically apply to all Copilot interactions
Supports Copilot Chat, Code Review, inline chat, and cloud agent
Complemented by path-specific instructions in .github/instructions/NAME.instructions.md with glob-pattern frontmatter
Priority system: personal instructions > repository instructions > organization instructions
Automated generation via Copilot cloud agent at github.com/copilot/agents

squawkCodes.title: File Not Found
squawkCodes.description: Instructions must be at exactly .github/copilot-instructions.md — other paths or filenames are ignored.

squawkCodes.title: Priority Conflicts
squawkCodes.description: Personal instructions override repository instructions, which override org instructions. Conflicts may cause unexpected behavior.

squawkCodes.title: Length Limits
squawkCodes.description: Code review only reads the first 4,000 characters of instruction files. Keep critical rules near the top.

codeExamples.title: Repository Custom Instructions File
codeExamples.code:
# Project Conventions

This is a TypeScript project using React and Next.js.

- Use functional components with hooks, never class components
- Use `react-hook-form` for all form state management
- All API calls must use the `fetchWrapper` from `src/lib/api.ts`
- Write unit tests using Vitest with React Testing Library
- Follow the project's ESLint configuration strictly

instruments.proTip: When Copilot uses your custom instructions, the .github/copilot-instructions.md file is listed as a reference in the response. Check the References list to verify your instructions are being applied.

aspects: Instructions are automatically loaded — anyone with write access can influence Copilot behavior for all contributors
Code review uses base branch version, preventing PR-based instruction injection
Can enforce security practices across all Copilot interactions
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Instructions can encode compliance requirements ensuring Copilot-generated code follows organizational security policies.

instruments.name: Personal Instructions
instruments.shortDescription: Personal Copilot instructions that apply across all your repositories
capabilities: Personal coding preferences
Cross-repo instruction persistence
Style and convention settings

squawkCodes.title: Instructions silently ignored
squawkCodes.description: If the instructions file is too long or contains conflicting directives, Copilot may partially or fully ignore them without warning. Keep instructions concise.

squawkCodes.title: Wrong file path
squawkCodes.description: The exact path must be `.github/copilot-instructions.md`. Variations like underscore separators or .txt extension will not work.

squawkCodes.title: No conditional logic
squawkCodes.description: Instructions are plain text — you cannot use conditionals like 'if file is in /tests, use pytest style'. Copilot applies all instructions uniformly.

squawkCodes.title: Instruction injection risk
squawkCodes.description: If the instructions file is contributed by an untrusted source, it could steer Copilot to generate insecure patterns. Review changes to this file carefully.

codeExamples.title: Repository-level custom instructions
codeExamples.code:
# Copilot Instructions

This is a TypeScript monorepo using pnpm workspaces.

## Code style
- Use `type` over `interface` unless extending is needed.
- Prefer named exports over default exports.
- Use `async/await` instead of `.then()` chains.
- All functions must have explicit return types.

## Architecture
- Data access logic lives in `packages/db/`.
- Never import directly from `packages/db` in UI components — use the hooks in `packages/hooks/`.

## Testing
- Write tests using Vitest, not Jest.
- Co-locate test files next to source as `*.test.ts`.

codeExamples.title: Personal-level instructions via VS Code settings

aspects: Malicious instructions could direct Copilot to generate vulnerable code
Instructions are sent as context, so secrets in the file will be transmitted
Personal instructions may override repo-level security guidelines
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Add `.github/copilot-instructions.md` to your PR review checklist. Use CODEOWNERS to require security team approval for changes. Never put API keys or tokens in instruction files.

instruments.proTip: Layer your instructions: put universal personal preferences (language, style) in VS Code settings, and project-specific architecture rules in the repo-level file. Copilot merges both, so you avoid repeating yourself across repos.

instruments.name: Prompt Files (.prompt.md)
instruments.shortDescription: Reusable prompt templates stored as .prompt.md files in the repository
capabilities: Reusable prompt templates as .prompt.md files in .github/prompts/, invoked via /prompt-name in Copilot Chat
YAML frontmatter with agent (mode), description, and tools properties
Interactive input variables using ${input:variable_name:Prompt question?} syntax
Markdown link references to other prompt files and docs for modular, composable instruction sets
Can reference workspace files using #file:path syntax

squawkCodes.title: Directory Required
squawkCodes.description: Prompt files must be in .github/prompts/ directory — files elsewhere are not discovered.

squawkCodes.title: Frontmatter Required
squawkCodes.description: Prompt files need YAML frontmatter with at least a description property to appear in the command list.

codeExamples.title: Unit Test Generator Prompt File
codeExamples.code:
---
agent: 'agent'
description: 'Generate unit tests for selected functions'
---

Generate tests for `${input:function_name:Which function?}`
using `${input:framework:Which framework? (jest/vitest/pytest)}`.

Follow AAA (Arrange, Act, Assert) structure.
Generate 5-8 focused test cases with descriptive names.

instruments.proTip: Unlike copilot-instructions.md which applies automatically, prompt files are invoked on-demand with /prompt-name. Ideal for task-specific workflows — code reviews, test generation, migration patterns — that you want to standardize across a team.

aspects: Can encode security review checklists as reusable workflows
Shared via repository — anyone with write access can modify prompt templates
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Useful for standardizing security practices as reusable team workflows committed to version control.

instruments.name: copilot-agents.yml
instruments.shortDescription: Define custom agent personas with specialized instructions and tool access
capabilities: Custom agent definitions
Specialized instruction sets
Tool access configuration
Team-shareable agent personas

squawkCodes.title: Wrong file location or naming
squawkCodes.description: Agent files must be placed in `.github/agents/` with the `.agent.md` extension. Files named `.yml` or placed elsewhere will not be discovered by Copilot.

squawkCodes.title: Invalid YAML frontmatter
squawkCodes.description: The YAML block between `---` delimiters must be valid YAML. A missing colon, bad indentation, or unquoted special character will cause the agent to fail to load silently.

squawkCodes.title: Tool availability varies by IDE
squawkCodes.description: Not all tools listed in frontmatter are available in every environment. For example, `terminal` may not be available in github.com web chat.

squawkCodes.title: Overprivileged agent tools
squawkCodes.description: An agent with `terminal` access can execute arbitrary commands. Granting `terminal` to an agent invoked on untrusted code could allow command injection through crafted file contents.

codeExamples.title: Security review agent definition (.agent.md)
codeExamples.code:
---
description: Reviews code for security vulnerabilities and suggests fixes
tools:
  - code_search
  - file_reader
---

You are a security-focused code reviewer. When asked to review code:

1. Check for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
2. Flag hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, XSS, and insecure deserialization.
3. Verify that authentication and authorization checks are present.
4. Suggest specific fixes with code examples.

Always reference the relevant CWE identifier for each finding.

codeExamples.title: Database migration agent with MCP
codeExamples.code:
---
description: Helps write and validate database migrations
tools:
  - code_search
  - terminal
  - file_reader
  - file_editor
---

You are a database migration specialist for our PostgreSQL database.

## Rules
- All migrations must be reversible (include both `up` and `down`).
- Use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` for index additions.
- Never use `DROP COLUMN` without a deprecation migration first.

## Workflow
1. Read the current schema from `db/schema.sql`.
2. Generate the migration file in `db/migrations/`.
3. Run `pnpm db:validate` to check syntax.

codeExamples.title: Agent directory structure
codeExamples.code:
.github/
  agents/
    security-reviewer.agent.md
    db-migration.agent.md
    docs-writer.agent.md
    api-designer.agent.md

aspects: Agents with terminal access can execute arbitrary shell commands
Agent definitions in public repos could be modified via PRs to introduce malicious instructions
MCP server configurations in agent files could point to attacker-controlled endpoints
Agents can chain tool calls — a compromised agent could exfiltrate secrets
securityRelevance.complianceNotes: Restrict `terminal` tool grants to agents that genuinely need it. Use CODEOWNERS to protect `.github/agents/`. Audit MCP server URLs. Consider review processes for new agent additions similar to GitHub Actions workflow approvals.

instruments.proTip: Invoke custom agents in Copilot Chat by typing @agent-name where the name matches the filename without the .agent.md suffix. Combine with slash commands — e.g., @security-reviewer /review — for specialized behavior without writing the full prompt every time.
